Read the spec. Summarized the key pillars: tracking-first, rich inputs to Markdown output, absorb deviations, planned vs actual, configurable coach, forward plan repair, data ownership, and ADHD-friendly without branding. Asked what to do next — architecture, prototype, pressure-test, or refine.
how do you imagine the app? high level

Described the app as one screen you live in during a workout, with planning/history/coach config tucked behind it. Three surfaces: Today (home), Plan (lightweight timeline), and Coach (plain-language settings). AI is invisible by default, surfacing as ghost targets, inline nudges, and a single "what should I do?" action. Offered to sketch a clickable HTML mockup of the Today screen.
make a wireframe runnable prototype use the multiple skills I added for you: ios-hig-design, mobile-ios-design, liquid-glass-design run it on my computere asap use sonnet agents to do the coding

Noted that only liquid-glass-design is installed; the other two skills weren't found under those names. Said I'd apply iOS HIG principles directly and use the liquid-glass skill for visuals, then hand the build to a Sonnet agent.

Built and opened a self-contained single-file HTML prototype in the browser. It emulates Liquid Glass with CSS, shows three working tabs (Today, Plan, Coach), and includes ghosted-vs-solid sets, coach nudges, and a working Export .md button. Noted this is a web emulation and a native SwiftUI version would be needed for true Liquid Glass.

no, build it as an actual ios app -- I have xcode and everything! don't use card containers -- those are for the web and look bad on iphone -- bleed-edge instead. I do circuits or supersets very often so make that representable on the UI. the UI should be tik-tok like where I only see what set I need to do next and perhaps cues or something that can be attached by the coach for the exercise. I should be able to tap next or provide very easily feedback on how easy/hard it felt or write a note or whatever. open it on my ios simulator when ready
Launched the app on the iOS simulator. Started taking screenshots to verify rendering.
Tapped Start to enter the runner and confirmed the main set page renders correctly.
Confirmed the superset page shows round info, a mini-map, and coach cues. Took a screenshot mid-scroll and planned to grab a clean settled frame.
Identified a ghosting issue where pages leaked behind the glass. Applied a fix and rebuilt.
Confirmed the ghosting is gone — clean single full-screen pages with opaque edge-to-edge backgrounds. Moved to verify a superset page was equally clean.

Confirmed the native iOS app (SwiftUI, iOS 26 SDK) is running clean on the simulator. Summarized the full feature set: TikTok-style one-set-per-page runner, full-bleed no cards, superset/circuit support with mini-maps, coach cues, effort feedback pills, and a fix for the safe area ghosting issue. Noted minor spacing issues and unverified circuit/Done screens. Offered to polish, screenshot remaining screens, add a rest timer, or wire feedback persistence.

yeah, polish -- the spacing feels off -- controls are also too far from the top and from the bottom of the screen. For stuff that's time-based (like a plank) I should be able to start the timer and see the countdown. Also, give me some way, maybe tapping the title thing at the top, to see the full list. Also, the reps number and - + buttons should be bigger

Queued all four fixes: rebalanced spacing with controls anchored to bottom safe area, a live countdown timer for timed sets with depleting ring and haptics, a top-pill button opening a sheet with all steps grouped by block, and larger reps stepper with ~52pt glass buttons. Said I'd rebuild and screenshot the timer and list sheet.

instead of a notes button, let me swipe right on the screen to go to a notes screen -- just like on tiktok you can swipe right to go to someone's profile -- same idea. But the notes shouldn't just be a notes, it should be a note but it should run the agent, like maybe I write "this felt kinda weird, it hurt my back a little" and maybe the agent writes back "ok, try with 50% less weight and tell me how it feels" and it would adjust the next set's weight, or maybe the agent decides "ok, let's skip this one for a few sessions and ease back into it in two weeks" and makes notes and whatnot to change the program, etc. the log & next button we can remove since I would just swipe down to the next exercise when I'm done.
